data.title: I'm Buying 2 Must-Own Dividend Bargains
data.description:
It’s a great time to be an income investor, with many names trading at well above average dividend yields. While it may be tempting some high-yielding stocks like Dow Inc. (DOW), their payout in excess of earnings is concerning, thereby making it unappealing for income safety.

That’s why conservative income investors may want to focus on large cap S&P 500 (SPY) companies whose yields are in the moderate to high range of 3% to 7%, which is what I would consider a sweet spot. This brings me to the following two undervalued and moat-worthy dividend stocks, both of which currently trade at well-below average valuations. In this article, I highlight why now is a great time to pick them up while the market continues to ignore their value, so let’s dive in!

#1: Verizon

Verizon (VZ) is a connectivity giant with wireless, broadband, and FWA (fixed wireless access) services covering the U.S. Beyond traditional retail services, VZ also serves enterprise private networks and emerging AI connectivity solutions. This is positions it to capture all market segments (business and consumer) while generating steady and reliable cash flow.

VZ’s steady business is reflected by Q1 2025 results, in which wireless service revenue grew by 2.7% YoY, reaching the high end of management’s guidance. This was supported by improved postpaid and core prepaid net adds Y/Y, with slowing decline rate to just 1% YoY. Retail core prepaid net adds continued to trend positive for the third consecutive quarter. As shown below, this combined with business phone net adds helped to offset the decline in consumer phone net adds.

Also encouraging, VZ continued to see growth in the broadband segment, driven by robust FWA adds of 308K connections, as shown above. FWA has been a growth engine for VZ as it has come with a higher premium mix than management had planned.

The slowing decline in overall net connections combined with pricing discipline and higher margin FWA growth helped VZ to achieve a respectable 4% YoY growth in adjusted EBITDA to $12.6 billion. These factors, combined with lower capital expenditures, resulted in robust FCF growth of 34% YoY to $3.6 billion during the first quarter.

Looking ahead, VZ is well-positioned to enjoy the fruits of its labor, as C-band deployment and fiber build execution are ahead of plan. Management expects to deploy C-Band to 80-90% of planned sites by year-end, and is aggressively rolling advanced 5G features. On the broadband front, year-to-date incremental fiber passings have reached 650K and the FWA multi-unit dwelling solutions is expected to gradually ramp over time. This could be a profitable growth engine as it could serve large numbers of households in apartment buildings. The expected closing of the Frontier acquisition will materially increase VZ’s fiber footprint. This will put it on a more even footing against rival AT&T’s (T) fiber buildout in recent years.

Notably, it appears VZ has taken a page from rival T-Mobile’s (TMUS) playbook as it’s ramped up customer perks. These programs can boost customer loyalty and stickiness through financial services. Management highlighted recent offerings during the recent earnings call:

We launched Verizon Access loyalty program, giving customers access to events like the exclusive presale of 100,000 Beyonce concert tickets, NFL, NBA and NHL games and lifetime experiences, such as the 140,000 free tickets we gave out to the first Verizon Super Bowl FanFest parties.

Our portfolio of adjacent services include industry-leading insurance products for both mobility and the connected home and give customers even more peace of mind. We offer even more ways to save through our financial service partners and products, including the Verizon Visa card and the open bank high-yield savings account with Santander, both exclusive to Verizon customers. This business is growing at a double-digit rate and bringing in margin-rich service revenue.

Importantly, the aforementioned robust FCF growth has enable VZ to continue to pay down debt. VZ’s net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio stands at 3.45x and I would expect for it to trend down toward 3x over the next 12-18 months. VZ carries BBB+ credit rating from S&P. This supports the 6.3% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 58% payout ratio. As shown below, VZ’s dividend yield remains far above where it has been over much of the past 10 years.

Lastly, I see solid value in VZ at the current price of $43.20 with a forward PE of 9.2. Like all stocks, VZ falls in and out of favor with the market despite providing steady results. It’s clearly out of favor with the market right now as its forward PE sits far below its normal PE of 13.4, as shown below.

Over the medium term, I would expect for VZ to achieve 5% annual EPS growth. This is supported by contributions from capital deployments and continued growth in broadband. Plus, management noted on the last earnings call that share buybacks are on the table after debt reduction. This, combined with the 6.3% dividend yield and even a partial reversion to the mean valuation, could result in potentially strong total returns for investors.

#2: Medtronic

Medtronic (MDT) is a leading global medical device technology company that serves healthcare professionals and patients in over 150 countries. Its four main business segments include Cardiovascular (cardiac ablation, cardiac rhythm management), Neuroscience, Medical Surgical (surgical robots), and the Diabetes segment, which is to be spun-off.

MDT stock has seen its share of ups and downs over the past 12 months, with shares trading as high as $96 and as low as $76. At the current price of $82.41, it sits in the bottom half of its trading range and just 1% higher than where it was at the same time last year, as shown below.

Contrary to what MDT’s lackluster share price performance might suggest, the business is producing solid results. This includes organic revenue growth of 5.4% YoY to $8.9 billion during fiscal Q4, 2025 (ended April 25th). Specifically, U.S. had the strongest growth in 15 quarters at 5%. Internationally, Japan had high single-digit growth, while Western Europe and Emerging Markets saw mid-single digit growth. Top-line growth was driven by positive showings from all segments, led by Cardiovascular and Diabetes with 8% and 12% growth, respectively, as shown below.

Notably, Cardiac Ablation Solutions sales grew by 30% YoY, reaching $1 billion in annual sales. Pulse field ablation products are driving rapid adoption with Sphere-9 catheter gaining strong physician preference, and Structural Heart saw 10% YoY growth driven by Evolut TAVR platform success. Moreover, Neuromodulation saw 10% YoY growth with closed-loop sensing technology leadership.

Management is guiding for full-year organic revenue growth of 5% for full-year fiscal 2026. Over the long run, MDT is well-positioned to benefit from expected fast growth in the Cardiac Ablation Solutions market, which is expected to grow in the double-digits annually. Surgical Robotics through the Hugo platform is also expected to be a growth driver company, as it’s now in 30 countries with growing procedure volumes. Growth catalysts are clearly in place for MDT with the growing aging population, as 1 in 5 Americans are expected to be 65 years or older by 2030.

The planned spin-off of the Diabetes segment could add value for shareholders as it’s primarily a B2C (business to consumer) business compared to B2B (business to business) for the rest of the portfolio. This enables MDT to focus on its core priorities while giving investors exposure to a fast-growing standalone business.

MDT maintains a strong balance sheet with an ‘A’ credit rating from S&P. This is supported by $9 billion in cash and short-term investments on hand and a safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.0x. This further supports MDT’s 3.5% dividend yield, which is well-covered by a 51% payout ratio. Medtronic is also a Dividend Aristocrat, with a 5-year dividend CAGR of 5.3%.

Lastly, MDT is solidly in value range at the current price of $82.41 with a forward PE of 14.7, sitting below its normal PE of 17.5, as shown below.

With growth catalysts in cardiovascular and medical surgical clearly in place along with the aging population, I believe MDT is well-positioned to deliver EPS growth in the high single digit in the medium to long-term. Combined with the well-covered dividend and a reversion to the mean valuation, MDT could readily deliver potential double-digit total returns annually from here.

Risks to Consider

Risks to Verizon include a competitive wireless industry, in which price competition could erode margins. Moreover, higher capital spending due to tariffs could also impede on bottom-line earnings. Risks to Medtronic include tariffs, which could hurt MDT’s sales in other countries, particularly in China. Moreover, tariffs result in higher costs for MDT. As a result, management has baked in $200-350 million worth of COGS impact for FY 2026 creating a 2-3% gross margin drag.

Investor Takeaway

For conservative income investors seeking reliable yield and long-term value, both Verizon and Medtronic present compelling opportunities. Verizon offers a robust 6.3% dividend yield backed by improving free cash flow. It has disciplined capital spending, and growing broadband and enterprise services, all while trading well below historical valuation levels.

Medtronic, a Dividend Aristocrat, provides a solid 3.5% yield with strong growth catalysts in cardiac solutions and surgical robotics. It has catalysts from an aging population and a potential value-enhancing diabetes spin-off. Both stocks are undervalued relative to their historical averages and poised for steady earnings growth. As such, now may be an ideal time to pick up shares before the market fully recognizes their upside.

data.title: AutoZone: Double-Digit Growth Amidst A Slowing Economy Makes It A Long-Term Buy
data.description:
Introduction

It's been years since I've set foot in an AutoZone, Inc. (NYSE:AZO) as my car restoration days are over. But during my time spent buying auto parts & accessories in their stores, I never thought of them as a wealth compounder.

However, that is exactly what AZO has been, and likely why the stock typically trades at such a high premium.

And listening to their plans for growth during their latest earnings, which I'll discuss later in the article, it doesn't seem like the company is slowing down anytime soon, which is why I'm reiterating my Buy rating.

In this article, I'll discuss AutoZone's latest earnings, fundamentals, solid growth amidst heightened economic uncertainty, and why AZO's strong growth is likely to continue for the foreseeable future, making them a must-own for wealth compounding.

Previous Thesis

It's been closer to 2 years since I last covered AutoZone in an article titled: Why A Dividend Could Be In Its Future.

AutoZone, known for their robust capital, has been aggressively buying back its shares in recent years. This, along with their impressive growth in free cash flow, growing from $903 million in 2012 to $2.12 billion in 2023, made me suspect that a dividend could be in AZO's not too distant future.

Although the auto parts retailer has yet to pay one, they've continued their strong growth trajectory despite facing economic challenges. Since my last thesis, the stock is up over 48%, impressively outperforming the S&P (SP500), up 38.22% at the time of writing, showing why AZO is a true wealth generator.

Q3 Earnings Post-View

AutoZone reported their Q3 earnings recently with a beat on their top line and a miss on their bottom (line). Sales amounted to $4.46 billion, beating analysts' estimates by $40 million, while EPS amounted to $35.36.

Due to foreign exchange headwinds, AZO's bottom-line saw a drag of $1.10. However, this still climbed from the previous quarter's $28.29.

Sales also climbed from $3.592 billion, up 5.4% year-over-year. This is impressive considering the challenging economic backdrop that has placed pressure on consumer discretionary income as a result of higher for longer interest rates.

AZO's impressive growth is likely to be attributed to their robust store growth. During the quarter they continued to focus on international growth, opening an additional 30 stores in Mexico & Brazil with an additional 54 domestically.

This brought their total international store count to 979 and management stated they expect to open 100 additional stores internationally, which remains one of the company's growth priorities.

In total, the auto retailer had 838 stores in Mexico and 141 in Brazil. And AZO is expected to spend $1.3 billion in CAPEX this year to continue driving growth for the foreseeable future.

Domestic commercial sales, which grew 10.7% to $1.3 billion, the first time AZO saw double-digit commercial growth since 2023. This was driven by DIY traffic increasing 1%, led by the holiday Easter shift.

Long-Term Growth Drivers

As impressive as AutoZone has been long term, I expect their performance to continue for the foreseeable future as a result of their international focus and satellite store openings.

As of their latest quarter, AZO only had 13% of their stores outside the United States, giving the auto retailer a long runway for solid growth.

And these should continue to drive sales & earnings, particularly due to their mega hub stores.

During the quarter, they opened 8 new mega hubs to bring their total to 119. They also have their commercial program at 92% of their stores and opened 49 additional ones for a total of 6,011.

For context, this is how AutoZone has grown since the first quarter of 2020. Below is a chart of their net income in total and on a per-share basis, sales, store count, and how commercial program growth has increased in the past 5 years.

As you can see from above, AZO's earnings per share and Brazil store count have both increased triple digits in the past 5 years. Furthermore, the auto retailer has plenty of room for international expansion and I suspect these numbers to continue rising in the foreseeable future.

Strong Growth Supports A Dividend

AutoZone currently prefers to repurchase shares as a way to return cash to its shareholders. And while some prefer this as this is deemed more tax efficient since shareholders are taxed on dividends, I prefer stocks who pay dividends as I plan to live off these in the not too distant future.

AutoZone's financials and fundamentals support the company's ability to pay dividends. The earnings growth has been impressive the past 5 years, likely a result of their aggressive share repurchases.

Since 1998, the company has repurchased 100% of their shares and during the latest quarter bought back $250 million worth of shares. For 2024, they repurchased a total of $3.1 billion worth of shares. Their current program had $1.1 billion remaining.

Free cash flow for the quarter was $423 million, which declined from $434 million in the prior year's quarter, a result of lower cash from operations. They also had 17.2 million shares outstanding, down 3.1% from the previous year. To show AZO's ability to start paying and safely cover a respectable dividend, let's use Meta Platforms (META) as an example.

The tech giant initiated a $0.50 quarterly dividend early last year. Let's say AZO decided to do the same. Using a quarterly dividend of $0.50, AZO would only need a total of $8.6 million in free cash flow to cover its dividend, well-below the current $423 million.

Even though their free cash flow decline was a result of cash from operations decreasing, this still grew slightly from 2020 that saw cash from operations amount to $2.72 billion.

Over the past 5 years, AZO's cash flow has been choppy, but still grew over time. Their balance sheet also supports the company paying a dividend with only $8.85 billion in debt, which actually decreased from roughly $9 billion last year.

This is small in comparison to their market cap of nearly $62 billion, showing their balance sheet is healthy. They do have $450 million in notes maturing in July of next year, but had plenty of liquidity available to satisfy this. So, who knows. Maybe AZO will follow in META's footsteps and initiate a dividend in the not too distant future.

Valuation

Although they currently trade above their 5-year average, AZO's double-digit growth warrants a higher premium, and I wouldn't be surprised to see the company trade near 30x earnings in the distant future as a result.

Using the blended P/E of roughly 25x, the auto retailer still offers significant upside, with an August 2026 price target of $4,225.00, significantly higher than their current price of $3,744 a share.

Risks

Despite their strong performance amidst a slowing economy, potential stagflation, increased recession fears, and current tariffs, AutoZone could see impacts from future tariffs. During the quarter, management addressed this during earnings:

During the quarter we saw minimal impact from tariffs. Going forward, there are several outcomes that may impact our results from tariffs, including vendor absorption, diversifying sourcing taking pricing actions or some combination of the three.

While it's possible for AZO to see vendor absorption and management diversify its sourcing, it's likely the auto retailer will hike prices.

And with a slowing economy and higher interest rates already placing pressure on consumers, this could result in slower traffic, negatively impacting sales in the coming quarters. If so, AZO could see sales decline, resulting in share price underperformance.

Bottom Line

While AutoZone could see selling pressure in the coming months due to a slowing economy and possible recession, their double-digit commercial sales growth is a testament to their strong upside potential.

Moreover, as their store count increases, I expect top and bottom-line growth to follow, resulting in solid price appreciation.

Furthermore, as AZO continues to expand and grow cash flows, I wouldn't be surprised to see the company initiate a dividend in the not too distant future.

Until then, AutoZone remains a must-own due to their wealth compounding and long-term upside potential.

data.title: monday.Com: Exceptional Buy As New Products And Upmarket Push Take Off (Upgrade)
data.description:
With the stock market continuing to hover around all-time highs despite cautious macroeconomic tidings, I continue to be very wary on where valuations are sitting. Especially as the Q1 earnings season featured many decelerating growth rates and cautious outlooks, I expect multiples to re-rate downward for many companies.

As such, I only invest in premium growth stocks on an exception basis in this environment: and monday.com (NASDAQ:MNDY) is one of those exceptions. In successfully growing a portfolio of disparate yet growing software products, the company is a viable contender to be the next major enterprise software portfolio platform, like Salesforce (CRM) or Oracle (ORCL) yet still in its infancy stages.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on monday.com in March, when the stock was still trading near $240 per share. Since then, monday.com has delivered powerful Q1 results that showcased a continuation of its >30% y/y growth rates, a meaningful guidance boost, and verifiable progress in new product initiatives. With a long growth trajectory ahead, I'm surmounting my earlier concerns on monday.com's valuation and raising the stock to a buy rating.

One of the reasons why I'm more willing to swallow a richer near-term valuation multiple for monday.com is that the company is now providing ample evidence that it has large ambitions to greatly extend its addressable market. monday.com was originally a workflow and project coordination tool - and yet now, the company is expanding into side areas like customer relationship management (CRM) and customer service management tools.

The company's recent foray into its service products bear special mention. In just over a year, monday.com has elevated a nascent product into one with ~700 customers (for reference, Monday Dev, a more established product, has ~4,000 customers: but Service also has the potential to grow as large as CRM, with over >30,000).

Now, monday.com's product suite encompasses CRM, customer service, workflow management, and product development tools. The stock is expensive: but there's an argument to be made that it can mirror ServiceNow (NOW), which similarly started off as an IT service management and later expanded into CRM and other categories.

At current share prices near $300, monday.com trades at a $15.10 billion market cap. After we net off the $1.59 billion of cash (against zero debt) on monday.com's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $13.51 billion.

For FY25, monday.com has raised its full-year guidance outlook to $1.22-$1.226 billion in revenue, representing a 25-26% y/y growth rate, up from a prior range of $1.206-$1.221 billion (24-26% y/y). It has also boosted its expectations of operating and FCF margins by roughly a point.

Against the midpoint of monday.com's latest outlook, the stock trades at 11.0x EV/FY25 revenue. Yes, a double-digit revenue multiple is expensive in today's market environment: but when we compare monday.com against other portfolio software companies like ServiceNow (which is growing at a ~20% pace currently, below monday.com), we find there is likely room for monday.com's valuation multiples to stretch upward.

Stay long here and keep riding monday.com's recent momentum higher.

Q1 download

Let's now go through monday.com's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Monday's revenue grew 30% y/y to $282.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $276.0 million (+27% y/y) by a three-point margin. Revenue growth also largely kept pace with Q4's 32% y/y growth pace.

Alongside expansion into new products, monday.com has been successful at pushing its business upmarket. The company also recently brought on a Chief Revenue Officer, Casey George, who previously headed sales at Qlik. In Q1, the company hit 3,444 total customers with >$50k in ARR, adding 243 net-new large customers in the quarter and growing the count by 38% y/y; the largest customer segment of >$100k in ARR, meanwhile, saw 121 net-new additions and grew at an even faster 46% y/y pace.

Meanwhile, the company has also been very successful at maintaining high gross retention rates and encouraging upsell. Overall net dollar retention rates held at 112%, which is an improvement over most of FY24. We note as well that the company's largest customer segment (>$100jk) had even stronger net retention trends at 117%, one point better than in Q4.

Larger customers are more likely to cross-sell into monday.com's newer products like Service, as well as to expand their seat subscriptions into more departments, so we are encouraged by the company's continued push upmarket. Eran Zinman, Monday's cofounder, noted on the Q&A portion of the Q1 earnings call that the Service product especially is geared toward being upsold to the company's enterprise clientele:

Traditionally, Q1 is a strong quarter for us in terms of performance marketing investment. And because of the nature of those products and the fact that it's a new go-to-market for us just the net adds that we saw in Q1 were high and kind of aligned with our expectations. With that, I would say service is not just an SMB product. We see 70% of ARR for monday service coming from mid-market and enterprise segments also strong momentum there. And for monday dev, we're very encouraged with the go-to-market. We're making some changes and optimizations and overall we feel there's good momentum. So overall, it's pretty much aligned with what we expected from Q1. Momentum is strong across all products and we continue the investment in terms of performance marketing going forward."

For me, one area of concern is monday.com's continued buildup in headcount to support its growth. It ended Q1 with 2,695 total employees, up 36% y/y. The company reiterated its plans to grow headcount by 30% this year (implying hiring ~750 people in 2025), at the precise time that many peers are utilizing the current macroeconomic slowdown and the benefits of AI to slow down hiring or even to execute layoffs.

monday.com's strong top-line performance still allowed the company to generate 14% pro forma operating margins, improving 4 points year over year. But eventually, I would like to see monday.com "walking the walk" when it comes to the promise of AI and making work more efficient.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are risks that we should keep in mind here. We've already mentioned expensive valuation multiples and rising headcount. We should also be aware of the fact that monday.com, despite its aggressive push into adjacent software markets, has fierce competition in everything that it does. Its CRM products compete against the likes of Salesforce and HubSpot (HUBS), while its workflow and dev tools are up against Atlassian (TEAM) and ServiceNow.

That being said, I see far more positives than negatives for monday.com as it continues to build out a fuller product suite and achieves fantastic revenue growth rates. Stay long here.

data.title: So Cheap It's Silly - 2 Dividend Stocks I'd Buy Twice If I Could
data.description:
Introduction

I've always been drawn to extremes.

Especially on the stock market, where the dominant story/narrative shifts dramatically in response to wild swings or significant changes in economic numbers and/or valuations.

Or to put it in simpler terms, we always try to make sense of what is currently going on. While I am painting with a very broad brush, this includes steep corrections and strong bull markets.

Whenever the stock market sells off, investors who would have bought stocks before the sell-off suddenly start to ask more 'what if?' questions. After all, when stocks sell off, something must be off, right? Better to wait before a new bull market starts before deploying more cash.

This doesn't just apply to new investors, but even to seasoned investors, as buying when stocks are down feels wrong. What if stocks continue to fall?

The interesting thing is that this question is always valid. Even during the best of times, we may be dealing with risks that are not yet obvious. However, because most people ignore them, buying feels great.

Unfortunately, the best buying opportunities happen during steep sell-offs.

As we can see below, the lower the valuation, the higher the odds that the market generates elevated long-term returns, as obvious as that may sound.

With that said, another extreme is making sense of elevated valuations.

  • In October 1999, the book "Dow 36,000: The New Strategy for Profiting From the Coming Rise in the Stock Market" was published. The timing could not have been worse, as it was a few quarters before the Dot-com bubble popped, causing the Dow Jones to lose nearly 40% of its value.
  • In 2020 and 2021, when the market fell in love with money-losing growth stocks, people made the case that the companies they owned were all onto something, potentially working on some of the biggest breakthroughs in their industries. They failed to recognize that these stocks were mainly fueled by dirt-cheap money and euphoric investor sentiment. Below is an example of a stock that now trades at $4, down from more than $120 in 2020, as it turns out that some people may have been a bit too optimistic back then, to put it mildly.

Currently, we are not in a very euphoric market environment.

However, it's still one of the most tricky markets, and people are trying to make sense of it.

As we can see below, going into this month, the S&P 500 traded at slightly more than 20.0x forward earnings, one of the highest numbers in more than two decades, albeit below its 2025 highs.

Moreover:

  • As we saw in the first chart of this article, the higher the valuation, the lower the expected total return going forward.
  • These numbers depend on at least 9% S&P 500 EPS growth this year and 13% EPS growth in 2026, as we are dealing with a forward-looking valuation. If economic challenges persist, growth could come in much lower. In other words, if stocks were to drop again because of a recession, stocks could still be expensive after a 10-15% decline.

Please note that I am not predicting a recession; I am just explaining the complex situation and the poor risk/reward that investors are trying to make sense of.

That's where John Stepek's Bloomberg article, titled "U.S. Recessions? They Are Just So Last Century," comes in.

He brought up something fascinating, which is the low number of recessions we have witnessed in the past few decades.

Roughly 40% of all days in the 19th century occurred during a recession. Over the past 16 years, that number dropped to a mere 1%, which is the short period during the pandemic, when we were all on lockdown, not knowing how bad COVID-19 could get.

Between the end of World War II and the end of the Cold War, the U.S. was in a recession 15% of the time. Since the 1990s, that number is a mere 5%. In other words, it's not just the post-Great Financial Crisis period that's favorable for consistent economic growth.

This is due to at least three reasons:

  • Since the Great Depression, the government has focused on avoiding recessions, using tools to prevent carnage before it happens.
  • Public spending has consistently grown.
  • The U.S. economy isn't as cyclical as it used to be, fueled by outsourcing a lot of industrial production.

I'm bringing all of this up because this could, technically speaking, lay the foundation for structurally higher stock market valuations.

Indeed, he’s very specific, calculating that in a world where nominal growth is reliably higher than in the past, the “fear” point for the price/earnings (p/e) ratio should be around 17, and the “greed” point should be around 30. With the S&P 500 on a p/e of around 24 currently, that means it’s neither cheap nor expensive.

However, Deluard also reckons we’re in for a bumpy summer, because as things stand, bonds probably aren’t quite priced for this more inflationary environment. If bond yields do continue to increase, then to get compensated sufficiently for taking equity risk, valuations would have to come down.

But if the p/e gets below 18, then in his view, it’d be time to buy. I suppose we may soon find out. - Bloomberg (emphasis added)

Personally, I believe there's merit to this thesis. After all, if structural risks are lower, this should be reflected in the stock market's valuation.

However, I also believe that this is already the case.

  • The stock market's valuation has been elevated since the pandemic, when it became clear just how powerful government spending is.
  • The Buffett indicator, which compares the market's total value to U.S. GDP, has spent more than two decades pricing in higher margins and structural benefits.

While I believe it's a very smart article, I think it's also the kind of article we usually see at market extremes, which is why I continue to focus on stocks with deep value in a market that is clearly not cheap anymore.

In the second part of this article, I present two of my favorites.

  • One is a low-yielding dividend grower that, I believe, is one of the best stocks for anyone looking to add elevated, anti-cyclical long-term growth at a fantastic price.
  • The other is an undervalued energy company that I expect to deliver double-digit annual returns for many years to come.

Essentially, I am providing food for thought for a wide range of investors today, including income-seeking investors and younger investors looking to buy growth at a very attractive valuation.

And with regard to my very bullish title, I truly believe these are two of the best opportunities on the market. That's why I did not make it a three or four-stock article, as most stocks on my radar simply don't match what these two bring to the table.

So, let's get to it!

Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) - So Good, We're Buying Weekly

Thermo Fisher has become one of my favorite dividend growth stocks.

However, I don't own it.

This decision is solely based on my choice to invest in Danaher Corp. (DHR). Danaher and Thermo Fisher are closely correlated, both serving as suppliers of high-tech equipment and consumables for biotech and other healthcare companies that require advanced R&D equipment and tools for diagnostics and various other operations.

As we can see below, they trade as if they are the same company.

However, Thermo Fisher is a core holding of various family accounts. For them, I bought TMO stocks instead of DHR, as DHR has a history of buying non-core businesses and spinning them off in new corporations after having improved their fundamentals.

As most family accounts are owned by people who are not financial experts, I wanted to avoid future spin-off decisions. By owning TMO, odds are they won't have to make any decisions if they want to keep or sell future spin-offs.

With that said, Thermo Fisher has a history of beating (crushing) the market.

Between the years 2000 and 2022, investors in TMO had consistently beaten the market. Despite losing more than 40% from its all-time high, TMO shares have still returned roughly 14% per year over the past 20 years.

Unfortunately, since 2022, the stock has started to struggle, pressured by post-pandemic inventory de-stocking, supply chain headwinds, and the trade war. After all, roughly half of its sales are generated outside the U.S.

During its most recent investor conference, the company noted that it quickly mobilized to address these challenges, implementing strategies to ensure a strong performance in 2025 and beyond, as demand markets have not yet normalized, and tariff headwinds are a major risk.

The good news is that areas like specialty diagnostics, pharma services, and bio-production are largely unaffected by policy changes. However, analytical instruments and lab equipment are facing some headwinds. This could translate to a 3% EPS growth headwind, which is a big deal, as China is a major growth market for the company and its peers.

While China is currently seeing declining sales, the company is seeing a recovery in the second half, which is likely to have legs, as analysts also see a path to double-digit EPS growth, as I will show you in a minute.

In general, while current headwinds are annoying (that's me putting it nicely), the company still operates in one of the most favorable growth industries, which it describes as "GDP plus," as the company sees 4-6% annual industry growth, likely leading to substantially higher revenue growth due to its growth strategy and relationships with major buyers.

As we can see below, the company expects at least 7% average annual organic revenue growth, up to 50 basis points higher margins, and factors like M&A to lead to mid-teens adjusted EPS and free cash flow growth.

While the company's dividend yields just 0.4%, it has a payout ratio of a mere 7% and a five-year CAGR of 15%. While this is a horrible dividend for income-focused investors, it will likely end up being a decent yield on cost for long-term investors.

Moreover, because of this favorable growth outlook, we're likely dealing with a company capable of generating tremendous long-term capital gains.

As we can see below, analysts expect just 2% EPS growth this year. This is in line with company comments and ongoing challenges. However, they also seem to agree with Thermo Fisher's expectations of a growth rebound, expecting 11% and 13% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

I expect these numbers to last if the company is indeed able to return to 7-9% organic revenue growth.

Now, the best thing is that TMO stock trades at just 18.3x earnings, a number I truly consider to be ridiculously cheap, as unprofessional as that may sound.

Even at its current valuation, there's a path to 10% annual returns. If we get a gradual return to its 20-year average P/E ratio of 20.1x, I see a fair stock price of at least $560, 40% above the current price.

At current prices and in light of the market's recovery, it is very hard for me to think of a better dividend growth stock than TMO, which is why most family accounts have added to TMO on a weekly basis, even the ones expecting to retire over the next five years.

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) - One Of The Most Underestimated Dividends

Just like investing in healthcare suppliers, buying oil and gas companies has been a struggle in recent years. As bullish as I am on energy stocks, shorter-term developments are painful for everyone expecting quick gains.

Global growth fears, tariff risks, and OPEC's decision to hike output twice in a market of slowing demand have pushed WTI to the low-$60 range.

Nonetheless, I'm very bullish, mainly because current prices simply aren't sustainable. As I wrote in a recent article, at current prices, U.S. shale production is unable to grow, and every single OPEC member except for the UAE needs additional funding to keep government budgets balanced.

OPEC is mainly boosting output to capture market share from American drillers and to punish members that have produced more than they should.

Especially if we get a cyclical growth rebound, I expect oil prices to average at least $80-$85 WTI, which is extremely bullish for Canadian oil and gas producers.

As I wrote earlier this month, with U.S. shale running out of Tier 1 reserves, Canadian players get both pricing power and volume growth benefits, all supported by the massive Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin.

That's where Canadian Natural Resources comes in, a company I prefer over almost any other major oil and gas producer.

Founded in 1973 as AEX Minerals Corporation, the company is the largest independent oil and gas producer in Canada, owning a wide portfolio of oil sand mines, thermal in-situ operations, and conventional production.

Unlike many of its peers, it does not need to protect its reserves, as it has abundant resources, allowing it to consistently boost volumes. Currently, it has a reserve life index of more than 30 years, one of the highest in the entire global oil industry. Also, this number has been consistent for decades, as the company regularly offsets production through new discoveries and M&A.

While most oil sand operations are extremely polluting (I cannot sugarcoat it), they provide a much-needed supply and come with zero decline rates.

Unlike American shale operations, where consistent well additions are needed to maintain steady production rates, oil sand mines mainly require elevated upfront costs to build infrastructure. Once that has been taken care of, these operations are very cheap, often breakeven in the mid-$30 WTI range.

Thanks to these qualities, the company also stands out when it comes to its dividend. In March, the company hiked its dividend by 4% to an annualized rate of C$2.35. This translates to a yield of 5.5%.

This dividend has been hiked for 25 consecutive years.

On top of that, the company uses buybacks and occasional special dividends to distribute additional cash, with special dividends mainly being used after steep stock price rallies, as buybacks are simply more attractive for long-term shareholder wealth generation at low valuation multiples.

After hitting its desired debt target after the pandemic, the company used to distribute 100% of its free cash flow to shareholders. Currently, these plans have changed, as the company bought Chevron's (CVX) oil sand assets.

This is the new plan, which is based on targeting a 1.1x leverage ratio at $70 WTI.

  • 60% to shareholders, 40% to debt reduction: Until net debt reaches C$15 billion, CNQ targets returning 60% of FCF to shareholders (via dividends and buybacks) and 40% to debt reduction.
  • 75% to shareholders: Once net debt falls between C$12 billion and C$15 billion, the allocation shifts to 75% for shareholders and 25% for debt reduction.
  • 100% to shareholders: Below C$12 billion in net debt, every penny of free cash flow will be returned to shareholders.

Under current circumstances, analysts see a path to C$15.2 billion in net debt by the end of 2026. With a bit of a pricing tailwind, we could be looking at a 75% payout ratio by then.

Also, because of the company's M&A, a 75% payout ratio returns more cash to shareholders than the previous 100% payout ratio, which means once it hits 100% again, it is in a fantastic position to return more cash than ever, even at subdued oil prices.

Here's the company's cash flow sensitivity overview:

  • At $65 WTI, FCF per share = C$4 (9.4% at C$42.55 stock price)
  • At $75 WTI, FCF per share = C$5 (11.7%)
  • At $85 WTI, FCF per share = C$7 (16.5%)

In other words, investors who buy at current prices could see 16.5% annual returns in the future at $85 WTI and a 100% payout ratio, which makes CNQ an incredibly powerful stock with massive shareholder return potential.

While this is enough to make the case that CNQ is very cheap, it also trades at just 6.4x operating cash flow, a number far below its long-term average of 8.3x and the 9-10 multiple I usually apply to high-quality oil producers.

All things considered, CNQ remains one of my all-time energy stocks and dividend payers, in general.

Both TMO and CNQ are Strong Buy stocks in my books, and I expect both to easily beat the market for many years to come.

Or to put it differently, short-term pain for long-term gain.

Takeaway

I've always been drawn to extremes, especially in the stock market, where changing narratives often provide very attractive opportunities.

Right now, we're in a tricky spot, as valuations are elevated, but structural economic shifts could justify higher multiples. However, I'm not banking on that, as I believe we're likely better off finding deep value than trying to make sense of stretched valuations.

That's why I presented two stocks in this article that stand out to me.

Thermo Fisher is a rare gem, as it's a high-quality growth stock trading at just 18x earnings, with double-digit EPS growth likely ahead. Even if its dividend may be disappointing, the company operates in a fantastic industry with a lot of room for future growth.

Canadian Natural Resources is my energy pick, a cash-flow machine with a 5.5% yield, 25 years of dividend hikes, and upside to elevated annual returns if oil prices recover.

Risks To My Thesis

Short-term pain before long-term gain applies to both companies, as energy markets are dealing with headwinds and healthcare suppliers are still battling post-pandemic headwinds in addition to trade risks.

While I believe the risk/reward for these companies is great, these headwinds could last longer than expected.

On top of that, general recession and stock market risks apply.

data.title: MBX Biosciences: Consider Peptide Developer With GLP Drugs As A Speculative Buy
data.description:
Investment Overview

In recent posts on Seeking Alpha, I have covered a number of companies developing drugs within a class most commonly referred to as glucagon-like peptide ("GLP") receptor agonists, or GLP-RA, or GLP-1s. These include posts on Septerna (SEPN), Zealand Pharma (OTCPK:ZLDPF), Metsera (MTSR), Structure Therapeutics (GPCR), and Viking Therapeutics (VKTX).

This post will focus on one of the more recent market entrants, MBX Biosciences (MBX), but first, let's consider why this drug class has become the subject of so much hype, and so much investment.

The best known GLP drugs belong to Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY). Novo's semaglutide is now approved in several indications - under the brand name Ozempic, it is approved to treat Type 2 Diabetes ("T2D"), cardiovascular risk reduction, and chronic kidney disease, and under the brand name Wegovy, it is approved to treat obesity, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

Lilly's tirzepatide is approved to treat T2D under the brand name Mounjaro, and obesity, and obstructive sleep apnea under the brand name Zepbound.

In 2024, Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro and Zepbound earned respectively $16.8bn, $8.1bn, $11.54bn, and $4.9bn. In Q1 2025, the drugs earned $4.9bn, $2.7bn, $3.84bn, and $2bn, up 18%, 85%, 113%, and 346% respectively.

Nearly $15bn of revenues in a single quarter may sound like a lot, but analysts at Goldman Sachs believe the obesity market alone will be worth ~$95bn by 2030 - and these are downgraded expectations reflecting expected downward pressure on pricing.

In a recent head-to-head study conducted by Eli Lilly, Wegovy and Zepbound achieved mean weight loss in patients of 13.7% and 20.2% respectively, marking Zepbound out as potentially the more efficacious drug, although Wegovy, which has been on the market for longer, remains the higher selling drug.

Different patients may respond differently to different GLP drugs, so mean weight loss is not the only factor - duration of weight loss, type of body fat lost, effect on lean muscle mass, adverse side effects, dosing frequency, oral vs injectable administration (semaglutide and tirzepatide are both injectables), titration, and weight gain after cessation of treatment are all important factors that will likely dictate the approval prospects and commercial success of any weight loss drug.

Only Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk have secured approvals for GLP drugs to date, but some analysts have speculated there could be as many as 16 new drugs on the market by 2031.

Although MBX is not exclusively focused on GLP drug development, as I'll explain in this post, its two candidates, if they produce compelling enough data, have the ability to drive its market cap from today's value of <$450m, into the billions, or potentially, tens of billions.

Conversely, most GLP-1 candidates to date have struggled to match the efficacy of tirzep/sema and therefore we must consider the possibility, or even probability that MBX's candidates do not succeed, which could result in the company rapidly losing value and potentially being wound up altogether.

Nevertheless, given the market opportunity, it is worth keeping a close eye on smaller biotechs developing GLP drugs, and MBX has a third prodrug candidate, which is not a GLP-1, as its most advanced asset, as I'll discuss below.

While the majority of GLP candidates may fail, as was the case when a plethora of biotechs were attempting to develop COVID vaccines back in 2020, for example, a single successful candidate could drive billions in commercial revenues, and make the biotech an M&A target for Big Pharma companies, with, likely, a double-digit billion price tag.

MBX Biosciences Overview - Hypoparathyroidism Candidate Data Catalyst In Q3

MBX completed its IPO in September 2024, raising ~$188m via the issuance of ~11.73m shares priced at $16 per share. As is often the case with biotech IPO's, shares had retreated to a value of >$6 by mid-April this year, but have since found some upward momentum, reaching a value of $13 at the time of writing.

Based in Carmel, Indiana, MBX discusses its business as follows in its Q1 2025 quarterly report/10-Q submission:

We are a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused on the discovery and development of novel precision peptide therapies for the treatment of endocrine and metabolic disorders.

Our company was founded by global leaders with a transformative approach to peptide drug design and development.

Leveraging this expertise, we designed our proprietary Precision Endocrine Peptide™ (the "PEP™") platform to overcome the key limitations of unmodified and modified peptide therapies and to improve clinical outcomes and simplify disease management for patients

Key differentiating qualities of its drug candidates include "extended time-action profiles and consistent drug concentrations with low peak-to-trough concentration ratios, consistent exposure to target tissues, and less frequent dosing", the company says.

As per a recent MBX corporate presentation, the pipeline looks as follows:

First, let's take a look at the most advanced stage candidate, canvuparatide, a prodrug - a compound with little or no pharmacological activity that metabolizes inside the body and converts into a pharmacologically active drug compound when triggered by drug-metabolizing enzymes (DMEs) - directed against hypoparathyroidism.

Hypoparathyroidism is caused by a deficiency in the parathyroid hormone ("PTH"), which can lead to cramping / muscle spasms, cognitive impairment, nausea and vomiting, and in some cases, lead to chronic kidney disease ("CKD"), or heart disease. MBX estimates it affects ~250k patients in the US.

There is not much competition in this field, with only one approved therapy to treat the condition - Yorvipath, developed by Ascendis Pharma (ASND), approved in August 2024, earning ~$30m of revenues by the end of 2024. Other treatment options include calcium and vitamin D supplementation, however MBX believes its once-weekly injectable may have the edge over rivals in terms of dosing frequency - Yorvipath must be administered daily, and potentially efficacy also.

There is not much data to support this thesis yet - although a Phase 1 study did show a half-life of 7.7-8.9 days, supporting once-weekly dosing, dose-dependent reductions in PTH to "levels at or below the lower limit of normal for PTH", and "mean peak-to-trough ratios of the active drug following the last dose ranged from 1.47 to 1.79".

A Phase 2 study is fully enrolled, however, and MBX says it will share data sometime in Q3, which is an intriguing catalyst worth keeping an eye on. The study has enrolled 64 patients, and is looking at doses of 400, 600, and 800 micrograms, and has a primary endpoint of "normalization of albumin-adjusted serum calcium while independent of active vitamin D and calcium supplements".

Given some analysts believe that hormone replacement therapy Yorvipath could achieve peak revenues of $2.5bn, if MBX's Phase 2 results are positive, its valuation ought to soar.

MBX - Obesity Portfolio - Post-Bariatric Surgery Candidate Set To Enter Phase 2

MBX's first obesity targeting GLP candidate is MBX-1416, although this asset is being developed as a "a potential therapy for post-bariatric hypoglycemia, ("PBH"), a chronic complication of bariatric surgery". According to MBX's latest 10Q:

MBX 1416 is designed as a convenient once-weekly therapy to reduce insulin secretion and increase blood glucose to reduce the frequency and severity of hypoglycemic events.

Preliminary data regarding the way the compound is absorbed, distributed, metabolized and excreted, ("pharmacokinetics") from the single ascending dose portion demonstrated that weekly subcutaneous injections resulted in dose-proportional increases in MBX 1416 exposure and a half-life supporting a once-weekly dosing regimen.

The second paragraph refers to the single ascending dose ("SAD") portion of a Phase 1 study, which also included a multiple ascending dose ("MAD") portion.

The study showed that the drugs were generally well tolerated, with no dose-related serious adverse events observed. MBX adds in its corporate presentation:

In mixed meal tolerance tests, MBX 1416 appeared to increase GLP-1 within 60 mins suggesting a PD effect in healthy volunteers that may translate into a therapeutic benefit in PBH patients; no meaningful changes observed in other parameters (glucose, insulin, c-peptide), as expected in healthy volunteers

Pending an end of Phase 1 meeting with the FDA, a Phase 2 study is expected to start in 2H25. The only meaningful competition - excepting restricted diet and off-label medications - is provided by Amylyx Pharmaceuticals (AMLX), which recently pivoted into GLP drug development. Amylyx' drug candidate Avexitide is expected to share data from a Phase 3 study in the first half of next year, after demonstrating "highly statistically significant reductions in hypoglycemic events" in a Phase 2 study.

Obesity: MBX 4291 - Most Ambitious Candidate Set For IND Submission

I suspect MBX 4291 may end up being MBX's most closely watched candidate as it is in development for obesity, and therefore has the greatest market potential. According to MBX the candidate:

is designed to be a long-acting and highly potent and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptor prodrug with the goal of potential once monthly dosing frequency and improving efficacy and tolerability relative to existing standards of care

Put another way, the mechanism of action can be described as GLP-1/GIP receptor co-agonist, which is a similar MoA to Eli Lilly's tirzepatide, widely regarded as the most effective GLP drug to have been developed (although, look out for Lilly's oral candidate, orforglipron, and its "triple G" agonist retatrutide, the former at the approval stage, the latter generating compelling mid-stage data).

MBX has stated its intention to submit its investigational new drug ("IND") application to the FDA in Q2 '25 for MBX 4291, although apparently, it has not been submitted yet. Once the application is approved, in-human studies can be initiated.

MBX-4291 is an injectable drug, however its expected dosing frequency is monthly, which, if supported by clinical data, would give it an edge over tirzepatide, which must be administered weekly - also the case for semaglutide, and retatrutide. Like canvuparatide, its status as a prodrug may give it a substantially longer half-life than rivals.

At present, however, these assumptions are based on studies in non-human primates ("NHPs") - these studies have also shown similar binding qualities to tirzepatide, and "similar reductions in body weight and food intake" as tirzepatide, MBX says.

Analysis - Is An Investment In MBX Worthwhile Today?

MBX made a net loss of $(62m) in 2024, or $(5.82) per share, but it had $262m cash as of the end of 2024. In Q1 2025, net loss was $(23.9m), or $(0.71) per share, with R&D expenses of $22.4m, and G&A expenses of $4.1m. The cash position was reported as $241m, with $233m of working capital. Liabilities were reported at $10.5m, and the accumulated deficit - the amount of funds raised from investors - as $(161m).

As a <$500m market cap biotech, investors need to be aware of the high level of risk associated with such companies. While the company's cash ought to last a couple more years based on current rate of cash burn, if assets move into later stage studies, costs will escalate, and more money will need to be raised, likely through at the market fundraising activities, that will dilute the value of existing investors' holdings.

There are significant catalysts upcoming with the release of the Phase 2 canvuparatide data, potential Phase 2 initiation of MBX-1416 in 2H25, and perhaps a Phase 1 initiation for MBX-4291 also, subject to IND submission and approval.

To date, I have tended to give GLP drug developing biotechs 'Hold" ratings, either because their assets are at the Phase 1/2 clinical study stage - these studies have smaller patient populations, tend to be shorter, and are therefore difficult to draw conclusions from - or because later stage studies have not established superiority or even equivalence to current standards of care - e.g. tirzepatide / semaglutide - or because their shares are expensive, and the risk / reward doubtful.

I have awarded Septerna, Zealand, and Metsera hold ratings, and awarded Viking Therapeutics and Structure Therapeutics "Buy" ratings, mainly due to the former's advanced stage of development, and the latter's strong cash position and experienced management.

MBX's management team also has some good-looking credentials. Its CEO formerly founded RNA specialist Avidity Biosciences, developer of Antibody Oligonucleotide Conjugates, and co-founded MB2 LLC, a "clinical stage company focused on diabetes and obesity, which was acquired by Novo Nordisk. His company bio also states he co-founded Marcadia Biotech Inc, acquired by Swiss Pharma giant Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY).

So far in this note I have not mentioned the Big Pharmas hoping to develop GLP drugs besides Lilly and Novo. These include Roche, which has numerous candidates and a tie-up with Zealand, Merck (MRK), which has acquired assets from a Chinese Pharma, AstraZeneca (AZN), which has done the same, and Amgen (AMGN), whose Mari-tide candidate is in Phase 2 studies.

It is interesting to ponder if MBX's endgame is an M&A deal with a Big Pharma. Chief Medical Officer Sam Azoulay was formerly at Roivant Sciences (ROIV), a company that recently sold its subsidiary Televant, developer of a bowel disease drug, to Roche for >$7bn (although Azoulat left the company several years prior).

The reason I have been sceptical on prospects for early-stage biotechs developing GLP drugs is that only Lilly and Novo have had genuine success in this field. Pfizer (PFE) recently gave up on its oral GLP candidate danuglipron, and even Novo has struggled with its amylin targeting candidate Cagrisema.

Semaglutide and tirzepatide have been termed "miracle" weight loss drugs, and matching or exceeding the high bar they have set is a tough task, that I would compare to developing a COVID vaccine to compete with Pfizer / BioNTech's >90% effective COVID vaccine, or Moderna's equally effective SpikeVax. Hundreds of companies tried to do so, but none succeeded.

The buy recommendations I have made I would describe as lower conviction buys, because we are still in the early stages of the GLP era, and it is tricky to state with conviction what will be successful and what won't.

One fact that makes me somewhat bullish on MBX however is its >$500m valuation, and the upcoming Phase 2 data catalyst for canvuparatide in Hypoparathyroidism. With only a single other approved drug in this market, with >$2bn peak revenue expectations, a strong data set would be a major upside catalyst, in my view, before we even get to the GLP programs.

Investors should be aware that any investment into MBX is a risky one, as the company has limited cash, and its candidates are early state - even canvuparatide will need to negotiate a Phase 3 study before it can be considered for approval, which could take two more years.

Nevertheless, on a risk / reward basis, MBX looks a tad more value for money than some of its rivals, in my view. Its prodrug approach is unique and differentiated, its management team experienced, its preclinical data intriguing, its cash position solid, there are near-term catalysts in play, asset diversification, and the company is a potential M&A target for a larger pharma - with a price tag in the billions, if any of its three candidates deliver data that suggests a "best-in-class" profile.

data.title: AI Infrastructure Divide Defines Generative AI Success - Highlights From VotE: AI And Machine Learning
data.description:
Introduction

AI workload demands continue to expand, with generative AI a driver of further investment in infrastructure. As organizations shift from experimenting with generative AI capabilities toward operationalizing them, new pressures are emerging on an already stretched infrastructure setup at many organizations. 451 Research's Voice of the Enterprise: AI & Machine Learning, Infrastructure 2024 study, a survey of more than 700 mid-level and senior IT and line-of-business professionals, provides a detailed view into the strategies organizations are engaging in to meet this pressure. The study explores AI execution venues, tools, purchasing strategies and challenges.

The Take

The opportunity for generative AI is well acknowledged. Almost all organizations perceive staff to be using generative AI capabilities, although in many instances the tools being used are not sanctioned or integrated offerings. Few organizations are in a position where experimentation with generative AI has led to embedded and broadly deployed capabilities. Considering the security and privacy risks of staff using tools without adequate levels of observation or controls, the pressure for organizations to rapidly deploy capabilities is sizeable. This pressure is further exacerbated by challenges of data theft and inaccurate responses associated with generative AI, and the limited value that can be achieved with generations that are not suitably tailored or anchored in enterprise data. As the dust starts to settle on the generative AI explosion, a small cohort of generative AI leaders appears to be leading the pack. These leaders have invested in a more robust AI infrastructure setup.

Summary of findings

AI workload and data requirements are on the rise. The vast majority of respondents (83%) forecast that AI workload demands would increase this year. Unsurprisingly, this increase is echoed by the majority of respondents who predicted that data needed to train models would increase by at least 25% in the next 12 months. Fourteen percent of respondents expect data needs would increase by more than 50%. Notably, the data assets organizations are set to employ are increasingly diverse — with significant and growing interest in leveraging unstructured rich media and textual data. Rearchitecting to account for rising demand for data, and the storage and effective use of new data types, will likely be a challenge for many organizations.

Generative AI is a driver of this multimedia trend. Developments in areas such as image and video generation, and a desire for tailored outputs, will likely encourage companies to rethink their storage strategies. "Data types" and "data volumes" are two of the top four drivers of organizations employing a distinct strategy for generative AI. These organizations, which represent the majority of our respondent base, also see the cost and security implications of the technology trend as major considerations. Investment in generative AI also appears to be having an impact on confidence that organizations have the infrastructure in place to meet AI workload demand. The proportion of respondents suggesting their IT environment was able to meet future AI demands without upgrades falls to a low of 29%.

Most organizations are still experimenting with generative AI. Few organizations are equipped to take full advantage of generative AI. While 73% of respondents are from organizations that have some level of investment in generative AI, the vast majority are either still trialing capabilities or have a limited rollout isolated to a team or project. Just 18% of respondents are from organizations that have integrated generative AI across the business, and these organizations represent a quite different profile. Respondents from organizations that have rapidly accelerated down the generative AI road map are far more confident that their organization's IT environment is able to consistently meet ML workload demands. These organizations make use of a wider range of execution venues for data preparation, model training and inference. In addition, respondents from these organizations are less likely to be concerned by perceived security or reliability challenges. Compared with organizations that have more limited deployments, generative AI leaders are also less likely to have a shortage of graphics processing units or storage capacity.

AI road maps are expensive to deliver. Budget has emerged as the number one driver of project failure as organizations struggle with the costs of AI initiatives. More than one-third of respondents (35%) suggest that budget considerations led to AI project abandonment in the past 12 months — a significantly higher proportion than longstanding areas of challenge like executive support (21%) or inadequate tooling (19%). After security, cost is the largest factor that influences AI infrastructure decision-making.

Trying to rein in AI infrastructure expenditure will prove challenging in the context of the broad upgrades enterprises have planned. More than one-third of respondents perceive infrastructure limitations as preventing their organizations keeping pace with AI advancements (37%), and a similar proportion see infrastructure limitations as a restraint on model performance (36%). Accordingly, spending on computing devices, AI accelerators, storage, networking and CPU resources is forecast to increase markedly within the next 12 months, and few organizations (11%) predict overall spending will do anything other than increase. For 10% of respondents, organizational spending on AI infrastructure is forecast to increase by more than 50%.

The growing importance of AI has remodeled infrastructure purchasing practices and priorities. Growing spending has elevated the importance of AI infrastructure decision-making within organizations. Paired with the broad applicability of AI across the business, this trend has contributed to a greater range of stakeholders being brought into purchasing decisions. Operations, finance and information security management roles are emerging as infrastructure influencers, with representatives of these functions being brought into purchasing decisions, while rarely being the primary decision-maker.

Sustainability appears to be taking a backseat, as the perceived importance of AI has led to organizations feeling a need to build out infrastructure quickly that meets security, cost and reliability requirements. Sustainability, while still seen as a key factor by 33% of respondents, is also seen as less critical than data privacy, flexibility and scalability requirements. The proportion of organizations that have invested in public cloud for AI workloads that suggest their decision-making around region or availability zone was a primary decision factor fell significantly year-over-year. In 2023, the majority of respondents said sustainability was a factor in region or availability zone selection (64%), and they made a decision because of it. This figure falls to just 48% this year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 3 Deep-Value Blue Chips: The Fat Pitch I Couldn't Resist
data.description:
The headlines can change by the day (or even the hour), so I'll be discussing the trade news in my upcoming economic update.

I'll explain why the latest news is excellent... but also not a panacea, as some might hope:

Federal Circuit temporarily stays Court of International Trade ruling to block IEEPA tariffs, pending appeal -- Bloomberg" - FactSet

  • Headlines only indicate that the Federal Circuit says it needs more time to consider the appeal.
  • Note that the Justice Department stated in a filing earlier today that if the Appeals Court didn't grant a temporary stay during the appeal process, then the government would seek SCOTUS review on Friday.

Because the headlines are in flux, I won't waste your (or my) time with a detailed analysis of something that might not be applicable within a few hours.

Short And Sweet: The Worst-Case Is Off The Table

There is a lot of good news.

At the peak of global tariffs on April 9, global tariffs were 30%.

They later fell to 26% after the "pause" and then fell even lower, to 15%, after the US and China agreed to reduce tariffs for 90 days.

Morningstar's latest estimates for global weighted tariffs, excluding the Liberation Day tariffs, are 7%.

That's about three times higher than global tariffs at the start of the year, but far better than an 11 times increase at the peak of the crisis.

For example, the difference between 30% tariffs and 7% tariffs is approximately a 2.3% impact on GDP growth.

For an economy growing at 2.5% at the start of the year, a 30% global tariff means a near certainty of recession.

A 7% tariff means a likely decline to around 1.8% growth (which is long-term trend growth for the US).

The US economy has been growing at 1.9% for the last two weeks based on daily and weekly reports from the Dallas Fed, which cover items such as daily Treasury tax receipts and weekly gasoline, steel, and electricity consumption, among others.

In other words, yes, tariffs mean slower growth. But it likely means that recession is now a much lower threat.

The risk of recession had risen to 75% at its intraday peak and has fallen to 37% in real time, according to betting markets.

  • The blue-chip economists, such as those at Moody's, agree with the betting markets; thus, this is my new favorite real-time proxy.
  • My official recession forecast aligns with the betting markets.
  • I am not smarter than the collective wisdom of everyone.

For context, the risk of recession in any given year is not zero. It's 15% because, historically, we have a recession every six years.

Why does recession risk matter?

Because historically, whether or not strong rallies like we've enjoyed in the past six weeks are "bear market rallies" or new bull markets (was April 7 the bottom?) comes down to whether or not we get a recession and negative earnings growth.

A 30% global tariff? Devastating for earnings.

A 7% global tariff? That companies can adapt to.

  • A 0.7% increase in CPI inflation would cover it vs. a 3% increase on April 9.

It's the difference between 2.2% peak real-time inflation and 4.5%.

And that makes a significant difference for consumer confidence, consumer spending, and whether companies have to lay off workers.

Now that the recession is a much lower risk (nearly zero, unless tariffs are reinstated somehow), we can say with very high confidence that the April 7 lows are the lows and the start of a new bull market.

What Should I Buy Now If I've Been Waiting for New Lower Prices?

Despite the scary headlines (and charts) in early April (and they were some of the most terrifying I've ever seen), I was very bullish during the trade war crisis for one simple reason.

The Most Important Bear Market Chart You'll Ever See

VIX Threshold Number of Days % of Trading Days Notable Periods Forward 1-Year S&P Return Forward 5-Year S&P Return 5-Year CAGR

Unless the world ends, periods of market panic, as measured by the VIX volatility index, are the best times for regular investors like you and me to earn Buffett-like returns from blue-chip bargains... from index funds!

At the April 7 lows (-21.3% below record highs), history says that by April 7, 2026, stocks (on average) are 40% higher, and by April 7, 2030, they're 140% higher, a 19% CAGR (Buffett's historical returns are 20%) on the S&P.

Obviously, averages are statistical, and I don't make forecasts, only provide historical context and the best available, most likely potential outcomes.

  • Fundamentally justified consensus total return potential is what I call it, not the "price target."

That was back on April 7, when investor sentiment was near record lows and volatility was the 33rd highest ever recorded.

But what about today?

Have All The "Easy Profits" Been Made?

Stocks are up 22.4% from their lows so far, but that doesn't mean that "easy money has been made."

The easiest money? Sure. By definition, rallies that break off market lows are always the easiest money.

  • In hindsight, no one can consistently call bottoms.

But it's a market of stocks, not a stock market, and so let me show you three blue-chip bargains, two of which I already own, that you might want to consider.

  • One I bought this week.

3 Blue-Chip Bargains With Buffett-Style Return Potential

Let me start out by explaining the most exciting idea I've had in the last week, a classic Buffett-style deep value opportunity.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH): An Opportunity For This A-Rated Ultra SWAN

Buffett became a legendary CEO at Berkshire, which he bought in 1965. However, did you know that a significant source of that funding came from his gains on American Express (AXP), a deep value opportunity that had emerged just two years prior?

In 1963, American Express found itself at the center of the so-called "Salad Oil Scandal" when it had extended more than $175 million in loans, backed by purported vegetable-oil inventories, to a New Jersey trader, Allied Crude Vegetable Oil.

Credit officers had accepted warehouse receipts claiming vast quantities of salad oil in storage. However, when inspectors arrived, they found tanks filled almost entirely with water, topped by a thin layer of oil. This left AmEx on the hook for millions in bad loans, causing its stock to plummet.

Despite the shock to its reputation, AmEx’s core payments and travel-services businesses remained intact, setting the stage for a dramatic multi-year recovery once investors looked past the scandal.

This is what Buffett was betting on when he bought $13 million worth of AMEX in 1963. That might not seem like a lot today, but it was 5% of AMEX and represented 40% of his partnership's capital.

In other words, Buffett bet 40% of his partner's capital (not just his own) that AMEX would rise like a phoenix from the ashes and soar to new heights.

He was right. AMEX bottomed and gained 40% in the first year and 400% within five years.

When Buffett bought Berkshire for a $5 million tender offer in 1965, 65% of that money came from his profits in the AMEX salad oil trade.

Had Buffett not bet that AMEX was not truly broken or going bankrupt, he wouldn't have become the GOAT of long-term investing.

What does this have to do with UnitedHealth?

UnitedHealth is down 50%. Not just down 50%, but down 50% in a matter of a few weeks... just like American Express did during its eight-week crash.

A few weeks of terrifying headlines, insane price declines that seem unbelievable, and then...the price stops falling.

And then... well, that's for history to decide, isn't it.

Why I Bought A 1%-Share Starter Position

My Dividend Kings Colleague, Justin Law, did a masterful job summarizing the perfect storm of scandals that have cut UnitedHealth in half in just one month:

Rather than reinvent the wheel, let's summarize.

But by 2030? If UNH's crisis ends within five years? And it grows as analysts expect (now that estimates have been slashed for six weeks)? Then, investors could expect returns of approximately 244%, or nearly 25% per year.

Why I'm Confident UNH Isn't A Value Trap

Other than the fact that 30 analysts who cover UNH for a living say that its long-term growth rate is likely to be unaffected, and that I know that UNH's historical fair value is around 20X, why am I confident that UNH isn't a value trap?

Let's turn to the most conservative "smart money" on Wall Street, the bond market.

That's the smart money saying UNH will survive this crisis, with all the analysts who collectively know the company better than anyone, other than management, predicting it will likely continue growing at a healthy, historical rate.

Thus, I'm confident that buying a 100-share position ($299.87 cost basis) is the optimal decision for my family, representing approximately a $30,000 position.

Did you know you can potentially earn 17% CAGR returns on an ETF in the next three years?

Let me show you one of my favorite ultra-yield dividend aristocrats... which has nothing wrong with it!

Fundamentally Justified Consensus Return Potential
